label: Six Cycles of Equal Taste
description: Distinctive Drukpa Kagyü contemplative cycle named by Schwerk as a central doctrinal and meditative system alongside Mahamudra and the Naropa yogas.
aliases: ["ro snyoms skor drug", "Six Cycles of Equal Taste / Ro Nyom Kor Druk"]

label: Nature of Mind
description: Direct recognition of the mind’s empty-luminous condition emphasized in Longchenpa’s Great Perfection writings and in related Dzogchen exposition.
sourceBooks: ["Finding Rest in the Nature of the Mind", "Spaciousness / Treasury of the Dharmadhatu"]
sourceRefs: Finding Rest in the Nature of the Mind: Foreword by Jigme Khyentse Rinpoche; Translators’ Introduction; ch. 10; Part Two, “The Mind Is the Root of All Phenomena”; Spaciousness / Treasury of the Dharmadhatu: Introduction; Prologue; Cantos 1, 3-4, 8

label: Dharmadhatu
description: The all-pervasive field or realm of reality, treated in Longchenpa’s Treasury of the Dharmadhatu as the spacious matrix of luminous awareness.
sourceRefs: Spaciousness / Treasury of the Dharmadhatu: Title; Introduction; Prologue; Cantos 1-13

label: Marco Polo
description: Book opens by noting that his records do not prove a visit to Ladakh.

label: Diogo d'Almeira
description: Presented as the first Westerner to visit Ladakh; reported a land of strange Christians and monk-like figures.

label: Antonio de Andrade
description: Reached Ladakh in May 1624; associated with attempts to reopen or establish Christian mission in Tibet.

label: Francesco de Azevedo
description: Short stay in Ladakh; travel account is noted for description of King Senge Namgyal.

label: Giovanni de Oliveiro
description: Short Jesuit visit from Tsaparang into Ladakh.

label: Ippolito Desideri
description: Reached Leh on 26 June 1717 according to cited travel account tradition; stayed nearly eight weeks in Leh; described rope bridge, avalanche danger, snow-blindness, and called Ladakh the 'Second Tibet'.

label: Manuel Freyre
description: Travelled with Desideri; reached Leh; later abandoned the enterprise and preferred to return by Kashmir.

label: Samuel van der Putte
description: Said to have entered Tibet via Ladakh and continued to Lhasa.

label: Captain Turner
description: Noted sculptured stones and inscription walls on the route between Kashmir and Leh and near Leh.

label: William Moorcroft
description: Stayed in Ladakh for two years; explored Nubra, Zangskar and Dras; studied agriculture and plants; built one of the first systematic British accounts.

label: George Trebeck
description: Appears as Moorcroft's companion in the Himalayan provinces and Central Asia journey.

label: James Baillie Fraser
description: Described Leh, trade fairs, and trade links with Yarkand and other regions; emphasized commercial significance of Ladakh and Gartok.

label: Alexander Csoma de Koros
description: Lived in Zangskar, studied Tibetan with local scholars and monks, and began the work that made him a pioneer Tibetan scholar.

label: Dr. Henderson
description: Reached Leh disguised as a Muslim merchant; kindly treated by the Ladakhi ruler; likely one of the first Europeans to visit Skardu/Little Tibet.

label: G.T. Vigne
description: Travelled from Baltistan to Leh and on to the Nubra valley, then returned by the same route; also collected plant specimens.

label: Falconer
description: Visited Kashmir, Baltistan and Ladakh and came up to Dras, collecting plant species.

label: Alexander Gerard
description: Crossed from the Manali side and described the motion sensation on a willow bridge en route to Leh.

label: Lieutenant Henry Strachey
description: Entered Tibetan territory in 1846 and later served on the Tibet Boundary Commission, which operated out of Ladakh and Leh.

label: Richard Strachey
description: Travel records are used in the book to show Hanle and the route onward to Guge.

label: Alexander Cunningham
description: Collected and reconstructed Ladakhi historical data, trade statistics, and observations on people, houses and economy; his work became a foundational source for later scholars.

label: Thomas Thomson
description: Collected plant species from western Tibet through Ladakh to the Karakoram and sent important material to herbaria.

label: Wilhelm Heyde
description: Reached Leh in April 1855; described the town as standing on caravan routes with a large bazaar and fine old royal palace.

label: Eduard Pagell
description: Travelled with Heyde; part of the team that opened Moravian mission work in the western Himalaya and Leh.

label: Schlagintweit brothers
description: Spent about two years in Ladakh and collected plants carefully

label: Pierre Jaccard
description: One of two Swiss travellers who commented on the difficulty of Ladakhi/Tibetan speech and vocabulary.

label: Pierre Vittoz
description: Travelled with Pierre Jaccard

label: G. Knight
description: Described Ladakhi customs, women, head-dress, Leh as a 'city of the dead', Hemis monastery, Kargil festivities, polo and social life.

label: T.G. Montgomerie
description: Helped measure Karakoram peaks and reached Ladakh with a Tibetan/Lhasa caravan in 1865.

label: G.W. Leitner
description: Recorded sculptures near Dras, costumes in Kargil, the giant image at Mulbekh, Lamayuru monastery, and inscriptions at Khalatse.

label: Robert Barkley Shaw
description: Studied the Arghuns of Ladakh, customs duties and transport; set out from Leh for Yarkand in September 1868; later became British Joint-Commissioner at Leh.

label: George Hayward
description: Joined or paralleled the Yarkand/Kashgar exploration world around Leh and later surveyed routes leading to Leh on behalf of the Royal Geographical Society.

label: J.L. Stewart
description: Made botanical tours in Ladakh and later returned in 1912-13, collecting large numbers of plant species across high-altitude zones.

label: Frederic Drew
description: Produced one of the classic geographical accounts of the region, covering climate, settlement, economy, agriculture, social groups, houses and monasteries.

label: Ferdinand Stoliczka
description: Carried out essential studies in Ladakh and died near the Karakoram Pass in June 1874; later commemorated in Leh.

label: Forsyth
description: Visited Leh with colleagues during a mission while British authorities monitored frontier interests.

label: Golden Trotter
description: Named as part of the Forsyth-linked group visiting Leh.

label: Henderson (Forsyth mission)
description: Mentioned together with Hume as a member of the Forsyth mission who helped publish a plant list from Ladakh and Yarkand.

label: Hume
description: Mentioned with Henderson as connected to the list of 412 plants collected in Ladakh and Yarkand.

label: Jonson
description: Climbed mountain summits around Leh from 1871 to 1883.

label: Mr. and Mrs. Ujifarvy
description: Travelled to Ladakh via the Karakoram.

label: Francis Younghusband
description: Reached Leh in July 1889; repeatedly used Ladakh routes in Great Game frontier work and later Tibetan political missions.

label: A.D. Carey
description: Reached Leh in July 1885, then moved eastward through the Changchenmo valley; returned to Ladakh in 1887.

label: Andrew Dalgleish
description: Accompanied Carey eastward from Leh toward Changchenmo.

label: Isabella Bishop
description: Travelled to Leh privately via Zojila with three servants.

label: E.F. Knight
description: Visited Leh and left a vignette of chhang, devils' dances and the bazaar atmosphere.

label: Martin Conway
description: Led a sizeable surveying party up the Indus to Leh and brought an artist and climbers into the Karakoram context.

label: Dutreuil de Rhins
description: Travelled via Keria Oasis, the Karakoram and Leh.

label: Fernand Grenard
description: Travelled with Dutreuil de Rhins via the Karakoram and Leh.

label: C.B. Clark
description: Credited with contributing to knowledge of Gilgit, Skardu and the Karakoram, though not much is said to be known from Ladakh proper.

label: J.F. Duthie
description: Crossed the Zojila in July 1893, visited Dras and the Deosai plains, then returned to Kashmir.

label: Dr. Weber
description: Responsible for meteorological observations and the post office at Leh

label: S.H. Ribbach
description: Made a sustained study of Ladakhi culture, education, custom and ceremony over many years.

label: A.H. Francke
description: Produced foundational work on Ladakh and Indian Tibet; surveyed Buddhist Dard areas and major routes into Ladakh.

label: Malcolm
description: Mentioned alongside Weber as surveying Ladakh in 1899.

label: Sven Hedin
description: Visited Ladakh twice; described Leh as the last important place before Tibet, the Moravian mission, and the graves of Stoliczka and Dalgleish.

label: Littledale
description: Passed over Ladakh after an extensive trans-Central Asian and Tibetan journey.

label: Fletcher
description: Companion in the journey that eventually crossed Ladakh.

label: Bullock Workman
description: Reached Leh with Fanny Bullock Workman and pursued extensive climbing and observation in the greater Ladakh-Karakoram area.

label: Fanny Bullock Workman
description: Travelled and climbed with Dr. Bullock Workman and became one of the most visible women explorers of the Himalayan world in the book.

label: Deasy
description: Came to Ladakh in 1900 and later published Tibet and Chinese Turkistan as the record of three years' explorations.

label: Ransdel
description: Came to Leh via Khotan in 1888-90, but the book says nothing further is known about his Ladakh travel.

label: Bower
description: Travelled to Ladakh in 1891 with Dr. Thorold and attempted a northwest-to-southeast crossing into Tibet from northern Ladakh.

label: Dr. Thorold
description: Travelled with Bower in the 1891 Ladakh-Tibet attempt.

label: Dr. Karl Max
description: Ran a mission hospital at Leh and is described as the first fully trained missionary doctor there.

label: A. Reeve Heber
description: Gave detailed descriptions of Leh bazaar, Lamayuru, Dard/Brogpa communities, birds, Korzok/Changthang and village life.

label: Kathleen M. Heber
description: Co-authored the observations on Leh market, Brogpas, Korzok, dress, religion and village economy.

label: Rawling
description: Entered the Changchenmo valley in 1902 and began a second journey from Leh in May 1903.

label: Meebold
description: Came from Kashmir in 1905, explored Suru and Kangri region including the route to Da, visited Leh and crossed Khardongla.

label: Tsuyoshi Hino
description: Presented as the first Japanese to have visited Ladakh.

label: Filippi de Filippi
description: Walked up to Leh along the frozen Indus through deep gorges with a large expedition train.

label: Major M.L.A. Gompertz ('Ganpat')
description: Travelled extensively for over six months, describing people, roads, religion, settlement, food and economy in detail.

label: Giuseppe Tucci
description: Visited Ladakh repeatedly as part of wider Tibetan Buddhist research and reflected on crossing into Tibetan cultural worlds.

label: William O. Douglas
description: After Indian independence, penetrated into Ladakh and travelled as a passionate mountaineer.

label: Marco Pallis
description: Left one of the most vivid literary accounts in the volume, covering routes, settlements, monasteries, architecture, women, food and ritual life.

label: H.R.H. Peter, Prince of Greece and Denmark
description: Documented population, social groups, marriage, divorce, kinship, houses, food, tax and polyandry in Ladakh.
aliases: ["H.R.H. Peter, Prince of Greece and Denmark"]

label: Walter Asboe
description: Started a professional school at Leh and organized the Gospel Inn catering to travellers on the Central Asia trade route.

label: F.A. Peter
description: Proposed reclaiming new land near Spituk using an Indus water channel.

label: David Snellgrove
description: Visited soon after Ladakh was opened to foreigners and co-published The Cultural Heritage of Ladakh.

label: Tadeusz Skorupski
description: Visited with David Snellgrove and co-published The Cultural Heritage of Ladakh.

label: Tokan D. Sumi
description: Visited after the opening to foreigners and co-authored Ladakh: The Moonland.

label: Masato Oki
description: Visited after the opening to foreigners and co-authored Ladakh: The Moonland.
